result: The clock on the wall doesn’t tick; it hums, a soft electric whisper that counts nothing and everything all at once. I used to think time was a river, flowing inexorably forward, sweeping us along its current. But more and more, I feel it as an ocean—vast, deep, and layered, where currents move at different speeds, some warm, some cold, and the surface often lies. We float on it, occasionally diving down into memory, into those pockets of warmth where a single moment expands into something infinite. A childhood summer afternoon, the smell of cut grass and gasoline, the drone of a distant airplane—it’s still happening, somewhere, in the ocean’s depths. Our clocks and calendars are just flimsy rafts we cling to for the illusion of progress, when all around us, the past is still swirling, brushing against the present with its cool, familiar fingers. Sometimes, in the quiet, you can feel the tug of a different tide, a pull towards a moment you haven’t lived yet but somehow recognize. We’re not really moving forward; we’re sinking, slowly, through layers of experience, collecting them like sediment, becoming more compressed, more dense, more full of every second we’ve ever truly inhabited. And maybe that’s the real journey—not across, but down, into the weight of our own gathered time, until we finally rest on the ocean floor, still cradled by the deep, slow currents of all we’ve ever been.
